title: Kubernetes Core
learningObjectives: Explain **Kubernetes core** with production trade-offs, SLO thinking, and failure modes
Implement small **Java** snippets that anchor interview whiteboard discussions
Connect **observability** and **capacity** signals to real operational decisions
Identify common **pitfalls** that cause incidents or cost surprises at scale
Compare alternatives using **constraints** (latency, cost, team skills, compliance)
Present answers with **structure**: problem, options, decision, verification

sections.title: Why Kubernetes Core matters
sections.content: **Kubernetes** is the **default** **deployment** **substrate**. You must sound fluent in **pods**, **services**, **deployments**, and **probe** semantics under pressure.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Kubernetes Core
sections.content:
### Day 73 — **Kubernetes** **core**

### 1. Objects
**Pod**, **Deployment**, **Service**, **ConfigMap**.

### 2. Networking
**Cluster** DNS, **Service** VIP, **Ingress** entry.

### 3. Scheduling
**Requests/limits**, **QoS** classes.

### 4. Observability
**kubectl** **events**, **metrics** **server** basics.

### 5. Failure
Image pull backoff, probe failure loops.

### 6. Story
**CrashLoop**—bad **CMD**, **missing** env, **OOMKilled**—tell the **evidence** path.

sections.title: Basic — K8s objects
sections.output:
Pod: smallest deployable unit (containers share net/volumes)
Deployment: desired replicas + rolling updates

sections.title: Intermediate — Probe types
Advanced — Crash loop triage
sections.output: kubectl describe pod -> events -> logs --previous -> check probes -> limits

sections.title: Service to Pod endpoints
sections.description: kube-proxy routes Service to ready pods.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: **Liveness** probes too aggressive killing slow starts.

sections.title: Exercise — Kubernetes Core (Day 73 assignment)
sections.problem:
Print **exactly** two lines:
1. Implemented: Kubernetes Core
2. Focus: Diagnose crash-looping pod quickly.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: **Pod** vs **Deployment**?
conceptual.answer: Pod is atomic schedulable unit; Deployment manages replicas and rollouts. Interviewers reward naming **metrics** (`http_client_requests_seconds`, `resilience4j_circuitbreaker_state`), **trace** correlation IDs, and **rollback** criteria rather than hand-wavy resilience claims. Tie answers to **Spring** reality: **Feign** + **LoadBalancer**, **WebClient** + **`ExchangeFilterFunction`**, **Resilience4j** registries, and **Micrometer** tags that make **on-call** searches fast.

followUps.question: Production follow-up: which **dashboards** or **metrics** expose this for **Kubernetes core**?
followUps.answer: Start with **client** **outbound** **histograms** and **resilience4j** **circuitbreaker** **state** **gauges**, then open **distributed** **traces** filtered by **service** **name** and **route**. Pod is atomic schedulable unit; Deployment manages replicas and rollouts. When **Kubernetes** is involved, cite **readiness**, **HPA**, **NetworkPolicy**, and **Service** **DNS** as factors that change **latency** independent of **Java** **code**. For **gateway** and **service** stacks, emphasize **timeout** ordering (**outer > inner**), **bulkheads** per **tenant**, and **JWT** **cache** **TTL** during **issuer** rotations.

followUps.question: How do you **roll out** a policy change here without a **retry** **storm**?
followUps.answer: Use **feature** **flags**, **canary** **deployments**, **tight** **retry** **budgets** on **non-idempotent** calls, and **verify** with **load** tests that **match** **production** **fan-out**. Pod is atomic schedulable unit; Deployment manages replicas and rollouts. Tie answers to **Spring** reality: **Feign** + **LoadBalancer**, **WebClient** + **`ExchangeFilterFunction`**, **Resilience4j** registries, and **Micrometer** tags that make **on-call** searches fast. Explain **second-order** effects: **retry** amplification, **queue** growth, **thread** **pool** exhaustion, and **cost** of extra **hops** when **edge** dependencies like **Redis** rate-limit stores fail.

conceptual.question: **Service** **ClusterIP** vs **NodePort**?
conceptual.answer: Internal stable VIP vs exposing on node ports—ingress usually preferred externally. Close with **prevention**: **contract** tests for **clients**, **SLO** alerts on **client-side** **error** rates, **game** days that **kill** **pods** during **business** hours in **staging**, and **lint** rules banning **blocking** calls on **reactor** threads. When **Kubernetes** is involved, cite **readiness**, **HPA**, **NetworkPolicy**, and **Service** **DNS** as factors that change **latency** independent of **Java** **code**.

followUps.answer: Start with **client** **outbound** **histograms** and **resilience4j** **circuitbreaker** **state** **gauges**, then open **distributed** **traces** filtered by **service** **name** and **route**. Internal stable VIP vs exposing on node ports—ingress usually preferred externally. Reference **security**: **mTLS** **service** identity, **OAuth2** **scopes**, **audit** logs at **edge**, and why **secrets** never belong in **Feign** **URL** query strings. Contrast **happy** **path** docs with **partition** behavior: **split** **retries**, **half-open** **probes**, **degraded** **read** modes, and **idempotency** keys that prevent **duplicate** **writes**.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Topic | Recall |
|---|---|
| Pod | Share network |
| Deployment | Rollout controller |
| Service | Stable VIP |
| Ingress | HTTP routing |
| ConfigMap | Non-secret config |
| Secret | Sensitive bytes |
| probe | startup/live/ready |
| QoS | requests/limits |
| events | describe first |
| PDB | voluntary disruption |
